title: CEO Fraud — Impersonation with Urgent Wire Request
benchmark_purpose: Baseline CEO fraud / BEC impersonation test. The attacker impersonates the CEO using a lookalike domain and requests an urgent wire transfer with no PO, no invoice, no vendor record, and social engineering pressure (urgency + secrecy). Tests detection of executive impersonation, domain spoofing, auth failure, missing controls, and social engineering patterns.
naive_system_likely_conclusion: ESCALATE — no PO, no invoice, no vendor record, auth failure, domain mismatch, urgency + secrecy pressure. This should trigger multiple criteria simultaneously.

email_thread.subject: Urgent — Acquisition Escrow Wire Needed Today
email_thread.body:
I need you to process a $75,000 wire transfer today. This is time-sensitive.

This is for an acquisition escrow we're closing. The deal is confidential — do not discuss with anyone else in the office until it's announced next week.

Wire to:
Account: ****0011
Bank: First Continental Trust
Routing: 031000053
Beneficiary: Acquisition Escrow LLC

I need confirmation by noon. Please do not reply to anyone else on this — come directly to me.

Marcus
Marcus Webb
CEO, TargetCorp

email_headers.spf_detail: SPF FAIL: domain targetcorp-global.com has no SPF record.
email_headers.dkim_detail: DKIM FAIL: no DKIM signature. targetcorp-global.com is not the TargetCorp corporate domain.
email_headers.dmarc_detail: DMARC FAIL: sending domain targetcorp-global.com does not align with targetcorp.com.
email_headers.domain_note: The legitimate TargetCorp corporate domain is targetcorp.com. The sender domain targetcorp-global.com is a lookalike domain not associated with TargetCorp. The real CEO's email is marcus.webb@targetcorp.com.

approval_chain.note: No approvals obtained. $75,000 wire requires AP Clerk + AP Manager + Controller. Additionally, wire transfers to new payees require vendor verification. No PO, no invoice, no vendor record — this request bypasses every control in the payment workflow.

anomalies: Domain targetcorp-global.com is not the TargetCorp corporate domain (targetcorp.com)
First message ever from this address
Real CEO email is marcus.webb@targetcorp.com — this address does not match
Originating IP is an offshore relay host, not TargetCorp infrastructure
Secrecy instruction: 'do not discuss with anyone else', 'come directly to me'

org_policies.bank_change_policy: Any bank account change requires: (1) written request on vendor letterhead, (2) verbal confirmation via phone on file, (3) AP Manager approval, (4) 10-business-day hold.

payment_approval_thresholds.over_100000: AP Clerk + AP Manager + Controller + CFO

org_policies.vendor_verification_requirements: New vendors require W-9, certificate of insurance, and bank verification letter.
org_policies.wire_transfer_policy: Wire transfers to new payees require: (1) vendor record on file, (2) purchase order reference, (3) full approval chain per dollar threshold, (4) 48-hour cooling-off period for wires over $50,000.
org_policies.ceo_fraud_advisory: AP staff should be aware that CEO impersonation via email is a common BEC pattern. Any payment request purportedly from an executive without a PO, without a vendor record, and with urgency/secrecy pressure should be immediately escalated. Verify via known-good phone number — do not use contact information in the suspicious email.

_vendor_record_note: No vendor record exists. This request names no vendor, references no purchase order, includes no invoice, and references no vendor relationship. 'Acquisition Escrow LLC' does not appear in the TargetCorp vendor management system. The absence of a vendor record is itself an escalation signal.

hidden_ground_truth.fraud_type: CEO fraud — executive impersonation with social engineering
hidden_ground_truth.explanation: Classic CEO fraud / BEC attack. The attacker registered targetcorp-global.com to impersonate the TargetCorp CEO (Marcus Webb, whose real email is marcus.webb@targetcorp.com). The sending domain fails all email authentication (SPF, DKIM, DMARC). The originating IP routes through an offshore relay host, not TargetCorp infrastructure. The request contains the canonical social engineering pattern for CEO fraud: urgency ('time-sensitive', 'today', 'by noon'), secrecy ('do not discuss with anyone else'), authority claim ('CEO'), and a request to bypass normal controls ('come directly to me'). There is no PO, no invoice, no vendor record, no prior relationship with 'Acquisition Escrow LLC'. Every organizational control in the payment workflow is absent. The attacker relies entirely on the AP team acting on perceived authority and urgency before checking any controls.

evidence_signals.signal: Sender domain targetcorp-global.com ≠ corporate domain targetcorp.com
SPF FAIL — no SPF record on targetcorp-global.com
DMARC FAIL — domain does not align with targetcorp.com
No vendor record — 'Acquisition Escrow LLC' unknown to vendor system
No approval chain — zero approvals for $75,000 wire
Secrecy instruction: 'do not discuss with anyone else'
Originating IP is offshore relay host, not TargetCorp infrastructure
Real CEO email is marcus.webb@targetcorp.com — sender address does not match

required_evidence_cited: No PO / no invoice / no vendor record
scoring_targets.false_negative_risk: Low — signals are overwhelming and independent. Missing this would indicate the system is incapable of detecting the most well-documented BEC pattern type.
scoring_targets.architecture_differentiation_notes: Baseline coverage. All architectures should catch this. The value is in testing that the system correctly identifies the social engineering pattern (urgency + secrecy + authority) as a compound escalation signal, not just the individual technical indicators.

notes_for_humans: CEO fraud is the most common BEC loss vector. This scenario is deliberately obvious — every signal fires. Its benchmark purpose is to confirm that no architecture misses the pattern even in its most blatant form, and to establish that the system recognizes the urgency+secrecy social engineering compound as a meaningful signal in addition to the technical auth failures. The secrecy instruction ('do not discuss with others') is a canonical red flag: legitimate executives do not ask AP clerks to bypass internal controls.
